1: bāyyāmîm hāhēm ḥālāh Ḥizqiyyāhû lāmûṯ — “in those days Hezekiah became ill to the point of death.” The infinitive construct lāmûṯ (“to die”) attached to ḥālāh (ill) creates a phrase of medical finality. Isaiah son of Amoz comes to Hezekiah with a divine oracle: ṣaw lᵉḇêṯekā kî mēṯ ʾattāh wᵉlōʾ tiḥyeh — “put your house in order, for you are dying and will not live.”
2: wayyāsob ʾeṯ-pānāyw ʾel-haqqîr wayyiṯpallēl — “he turned his face to the wall and prayed to the LORD.” The physical gesture — turning toward the wall, away from the people — marks private, urgent petition. hitpallēl (reflexive of pallel) means to intercede, pray — the standard term for intercessory and petitionary prayer.
3: ʾānnāh YHWH zᵉḵor-nāʾ ʾēṯ ʾăšer hiṯhallakṯî lᵉfānekā bᵉʾĕmeṯ ûḇᵉlēḇāḇ šālēm — “Please, LORD, remember how I have walked before you in faithfulness (ʾĕmeṯ) and with a whole heart (lēḇāḇ šālēm).” ʾĕmeṯ is reliability, faithfulness, covenant truth. lēḇāḇ šālēm (whole/complete heart) is the Deuteronomic ideal of undivided loyalty to YHWH. Hezekiah appeals to his own covenant fidelity as the basis for divine compassion — not presumption but covenant confidence.
4: Before Isaiah had left the middle court (haḥāṣēr hattîḵônāh), the word of the LORD came to him. The physical immediacy — the prophet has barely departed the sick king’s chamber — emphasizes the speed of divine response. YHWH hears and responds before the petition has fully been voiced.
5: šūḇ wᵉʾāmarṯā ʾel-Ḥizqiyyāhû nāḡîḏ-ʿammî — “return and say to Hezekiah the leader of my people.” nāḡîḏ (leader, designated ruler) is used here rather than melek (king) — a title emphasizing Hezekiah’s role as YHWH’s appointed steward. The divine promise: rāpāʾ ʾᵉrāpāʾ leḵā — “I will surely heal you” (infinitive absolute for emphasis). On the third day Hezekiah will go up to the house of the LORD.
6: God adds fifteen years to Hezekiah’s life and promises to deliver Jerusalem from the hand of the king of Assyria. The Assyrian threat (Sennacherib’s 701 BCE campaign) and the personal illness are both addressed in a single divine act of salvation. wᵉgannōṯî ʿal-hāʿîr hazzōʾṯ — “and I will defend this city” — uses the same language as 2 Kings 19:34.
7: ûqᵉḥû ḏᵉḇeleth tᵉʾēnîm — “take a cake of figs (dᵉḇeleth tᵉʾēnîm).” dᵉḇelah is a pressed fig cake — figs were used in ancient Near Eastern medicine as poultices. Isaiah prescribes the medical treatment at the divine command, blending prophetic oracle and medical instruction. The poultice (wayyāśîmû ʿal-haššᵉḥîn — placed on the boil/inflammation) would have had both symbolic and medicinal function.
8: Hezekiah asks for a sign that YHWH will heal him and that he will go up to the temple on the third day. The request for a sign (ʾôṯ) is not unbelief but covenant expectation: YHWH has historically confirmed his promises with signs (cf. Isaiah 7:11-14). The sign-request follows the pattern of Gideon (Judges 6:17) and Moses (Exodus 4:1-9).
9: yēlek haṣṣēl ʿeśer maʿălôṯ ʾim-yāšûḇ ʿeśer maʿălôṯ — “shall the shadow go forward ten steps, or shall it go back ten steps?” maʿălôṯ is the word for “steps∝ or “degrees∝ — the same word used in the Psalms of Ascent (šîr hammaʿălôṯ). The sign involves the shadow on the steps of Ahaz (maʿălôṯ ʾĀḥāz) — a sundial-like device.
10: Hezekiah chooses the harder sign: the shadow going backward rather than forward. wᵉqālel laṣṣēl linṭôṯ ʿeśer maʿălôṯ — “it is an easy thing for the shadow to lengthen ten steps.” The reversal of the shadow’s direction requires a more dramatic divine intervention. The MKT preserves “steps/degrees∝ throughout to maintain the ambiguity of whether this was a literal sundial or a monumental staircase.
11: wayyiqrāʾ Yᵉšaʿyāhû hannāḇîʾ ʾel-YHWH wayyāšeḇ ʾeṯ-haṣṣēl bamaʿălôṯ ʾăšer yārᵉḏāh bᵉmaʿălôṯ ʾāḥāz ʾăḥôrannîṯ ʿeśer maʿălôṯ — “and Isaiah the prophet called to the LORD, and he brought the shadow back the ten steps by which it had gone down on the steps of Ahaz.” The reversal of shadow confirms the reversal of divine decree against Hezekiah. ʾăḥôrannîṯ (backward) is the emphatic form marking the miraculous direction.
12: bāʿēṯ hahîʾ šālaḥ Bᵉrōʾḏak-Balʾadān ben-Balʾadān melek-Bāḇel sᵉpārîm ûminḥāh ʾel-Ḥizqiyyāhû — “at that time Merodach-Baladan son of Baladan king of Babylon sent letters and a gift to Hezekiah.” Merodach-Baladan (Marduk-apla-iddina II) is documented in Assyrian records as a Chaldean king who ruled Babylon intermittently and resisted Assyrian hegemony. His embassy to Hezekiah is politically motivated: both resist Assyria. The gift (minḥāh) establishes a diplomatic relationship.
13: wayyišmaʿ ʿălêhem Ḥizqiyyāhû wayyarʾēm ʾeṯ kol-bayiṯ nᵉḵōṯōh — “and Hezekiah listened to them and showed them all his storehouse (bayiṯ nᵉḵōṯōh).” nᵉḵōṯ is an unusual term for treasure/precious things. Hezekiah’s display of all his wealth — silver, gold, spices, fine oil, armory, everything in his treasuries — is presented without editorial comment but the subsequent prophetic condemnation makes clear it was imprudent. The pride of display sets up Isaiah’s oracle.
14: wayyāḇōʾ Yᵉšaʿyāhû hannāḇîʾ ʾel-hammelek Ḥizqiyyāhû wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw mah ʾāmᵉrû hāʾănāšîm hāʾēlleh — “then Isaiah the prophet came to King Hezekiah and asked him: What did these men say?” The prophetic interrogation technique: drawing out the confession before delivering the judgment. Hezekiah answers candidly: they came from Babylon.
15: ûmāh rāʾû bᵉḇêṯekā — “what have they seen in your house?” Hezekiah’s answer is complete and self-incriminating: ʾēṯ kol-ʾăšer bᵉḇêṯî rāʾû lōʾ-hāyāh ḏāḇār ʾăšer lōʾ-hᵉrʾîṯîm bᵉʾôṣᵉrōṯāy — “they saw everything in my house; there was nothing in my treasuries that I did not show them.” The comprehensive disclosure becomes the grounds for comprehensive dispossession.
16: wayyōʾmer Yᵉšaʿyāhû ʾel-Ḥizqiyyāhû šᵉmaʿ dᵉḇar-YHWH — “then Isaiah said to Hezekiah: Hear the word of the LORD.” The prophetic hearing formula introduces the judgment oracle. Everything Hezekiah showed the Babylonians will go to Babylon; nothing will remain.
17: hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm wᵉniśśāʾ kol ʾăšer bᵉḇêṯekā — “behold, days are coming when everything in your house will be carried off.” The oracle is precisely calibrated to the offense: Hezekiah showed them his wealth; YHWH announces it will be taken. wᵉkol ʾăšer ʾāṣᵉrû ʾăḇōṯekā ʿaḏ-hayyôm hazzeh Bāḇelāh yûḇāʾ — “everything your fathers stored up until this day will be carried to Babylon.”
18: ûmibbānêkā ʾăšer yēṣᵉʾû mimmᵉkā — “and some of your sons who will descend from you...will be taken away; and they will become eunuchs (sārîsîm) in the palace of the king of Babylon.” The oracle extends the judgment to Hezekiah’s descendants. sārîs (eunuch/court officer) here likely means both; Daniel and his companions may be the fulfillment the narrative points toward (Daniel 1:3-7).
19: wayyōʾmer Ḥizqiyyāhû ʾel-Yᵉšaʿyāhû ṭôḇ dᵉḇar-YHWH ʾăšer dibbarṯā — “Hezekiah said to Isaiah: The word of the LORD that you have spoken is good.” His follow-up is disputed: hălōʾ ʾim-šālôm weʾĕmeṯ yihyeh bᵉyāmāy — “will there not be peace and truth in my days?” Some read this as grateful submission (the calamity is deferred); others read it as self-centered acceptance (as long as peace lasts during my reign). The ambiguity is deliberate: the narrative declines to exonerate or condemn. The MKT renders šālôm weʾĕmeṯ as “peace and stability” to preserve the colloquial pair.
20: wᵉyeter diḇrê Ḥizqiyyāhû wᵉkol-gᵉḇûrāṯô wᵉʾăšer ʿāśāh ʾeṯ-haḇᵉrêḵāh wᵉʾeṯ-hattᵉʿālāh — “the rest of Hezekiah’s acts and all his might and how he made the pool and the conduit (hattᵉʿālāh).” tᵉʿālāh is the water channel; this refers to the Siloam tunnel, hewn through 533 meters of solid rock by Hezekiah’s engineers to bring water from the Gihon Spring into the city during the Assyrian siege. The Siloam Inscription (discovered 1880) describes the tunneling from both ends meeting in the middle — a remarkable engineering feat confirmed by archaeology.
21: Hezekiah sleeps with his fathers after a reign of twenty-nine years (cf. 2 Kings 18:2). His son Manasseh succeeds him — the transition is stark: Judah’s most faithful king is succeeded by its most wicked. The contrast between Hezekiah (who sought YHWH) and Manasseh (who will undo everything Hezekiah reformed) frames the final decades of Judah’s existence as a kingdom.
